update_reason: Per HNOTE-OPERATOR-PRED-073-CATALOG-GAP-001 (2026-05-23): operator explicitly requested the standard-physics analysis include CHAOS-7/IGRF-13 baseline drift rates and a quantitative comparison against the dome’s claimed magnitude. The decider-2026-05-23-1850 stopgap entry assessed the prediction qualitatively (mechanism-not-required argument) but did not perform the numerical comparison. This refinement supplies the missing quantitative analysis and revises the testability_score and the likely-outcome forecast.
proposed_detail_reasoning_replacement:
SAA western-cell secular drift is one of the best-characterized geomagnetic phenomena of the modern era. IGRF-13 model output (derived from CHAOS-7, the same data source the dome cites) gives a western-cell westward longitudinal drift of 0.282 ± 0.030°/year averaged over 2015–2025 (Pavón-Carrasco & De Santis, 2016 baseline; Finlay et al. 2020 'The CHAOS-7 geomagnetic field model and observed changes in the South Atlantic Anomaly', Earth Planets Space 72:156 — see https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40623-020-01252-9). Independent confirmation from CSES proton-flux satellite measurements gives 0.33 ± 0.08°/year (dayside) and 0.33 ± 0.10°/year (nightside) for the proton centroid (Science China Earth Sciences, 2025 — see https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11430-025-1672-2). The cluster of independent estimates places the SAA western-cell drift in the 0.25–0.36°/year band, consistent with core-mantle differential rotation (~0.2–0.5°/year per long-standing literature).

The dome’s prediction is for ≥0.3° westward drift over a single month (April→May 2026, ~30 days). Annualized, this is 0.3° × 365/30 = 3.65°/year — approximately 12–13× faster than the IGRF-13 western-cell rate of 0.282°/year and ~10× above the upper end of the CSES proton-centroid 95% confidence band. Standard secular variation over April→May 2026 should therefore produce roughly 0.282/12 ≈ 0.024° of westward drift — about an order of magnitude below the dome’s ≥0.3° threshold (data/predictions.json:PRED-073).

The dome’s own falsification rule — 'SAA western cell drifts <0.1° westward or reverses direction' — is therefore triggered by the standard-physics expectation itself. If the IGRF/CHAOS-7 secular-variation rate continues (the null hypothesis given the past decade of consistent monitoring), the observed April→May drift of ~0.024° falls well below the <0.1° falsification threshold the dome itself published. This is structurally important: the dome has committed to a numerical claim that defaults-to-falsified under the consensus null hypothesis, not merely a vague directional claim that is satisfied by 'drift exists.' Compare to most dome predictions, which either restate observations or use threshold values inside the standard-physics confidence band (data/predictions.json passim).

The one caveat — and where the dome retains a thin survival path — is measurement-noise headroom. CHAOS-7 monthly maps have spatial-fitting uncertainty in the SAA region on the order of ±0.1–0.2° for centroid position (Finlay et al. 2020, cited above), so a measured drift in the 0.1–0.2° range would be ambiguous: above the dome’s <0.1° falsification threshold but still within the standard-physics noise floor. A measured drift of ≥0.3° — the dome’s confirmation threshold — would be well outside both standard secular variation and the model noise band, and would be a genuine anomaly. A measured drift of <0.1° (the most likely standard-physics outcome) cleanly falsifies on the dome’s own rule.

Kernel of truth: the dome correctly identifies the SAA western cell as drifting westward, and the direction matches both the IGRF/CHAOS-7 observation and the dome’s 'toroidal aetheric return flow' mechanism. The mechanism description even matches a real physical analogy — core-mantle differential rotation does produce westward drift of magnetic features, and the rate (~0.2–0.5°/year) is the right order of magnitude for both core-flow and aetheric-flow narratives. Where the dome fails is in committing to a magnitude (≥0.3°/month) that is an order of magnitude faster than the observed secular variation. The dome appears to have conflated 'long-term drift rate' (~0.28°/year, what CHAOS-7 actually measures) with 'short-term excursion' (~0.3°/month, what the dome predicts) — possibly by treating the annual rate as a monthly rate during prediction authoring. This is a numerical mistake the dome can fix at no cost by amending the prediction to ~0.024°/month, but doing so would dissolve the prediction into a restate-of-standard-physics with no discriminating power. The dome’s commitment to ≥0.3°/month is what gives PRED-073 real falsifiability content — and is precisely what makes it likely to fail.

Data availability: CHAOS-7 monthly maps for May 2026 are typically published in early June; SAA centroid position in the IGRF-13 framework should be derivable within ~10 days of release. The likely sequence is (a) May 2026 CHAOS-7 update arrives early June, (b) standard SAA centroid analysis tools (e.g., the Finlay group’s public CHAOS-7 release pipeline) compute the April→May drift within ~1–2 weeks, (c) the dome either posts a result or silently ignores. Per the broader dome-prediction pattern (PRED-072, PRED-093 stress-test history), silent-ignore on standard-physics falsifications is the modal outcome.
proposed_tldr: Predicts the South Atlantic Anomaly’s western cell will drift at least 0.3° westward in just one month — but the well-measured IGRF-13 / CHAOS-7 rate (0.282°/year) means standard physics expects only ~0.024° in that window, about 12× less than the dome’s threshold. Because the dome’s own falsification rule says <0.1° = failed, the standard-physics expectation already triggers it. CHAOS-7 May data arrives early June; likely-falsified-on-dome’s-own-rule.
reasoning: The decider-stopgap PRED-073 entry argued the dome mechanism is not required because standard core-mantle dynamics explain SAA westward drift — correct as far as it goes, but it missed the numerical asymmetry: the dome predicts 12–13× faster drift than IGRF-13 measures, and the dome’s own falsification threshold (<0.1°/month) is triggered by the standard-physics expectation (~0.024°/month). This converts PRED-073 from a generic standard-physics-dressed-as-aether prediction (low discriminating power) to a genuinely falsifiable claim that defaults-to-falsified under the consensus null hypothesis. Testability_score upgraded from medium to high; likely_outcome predicted (falsified-by-standard-physics, with a thin survival corridor in the 0.1–0.2°/month measurement-noise band).
kernel_of_truth: The dome correctly identifies the SAA western cell as drifting westward, gets the direction right, and the dome’s aetheric-return-flow mechanism is at least the right order of magnitude (0.2–0.5°/year) for what core-flow theory also predicts. The failure is purely numerical: the dome committed to a magnitude (≥0.3°/month) that is ~12× faster than the IGRF-13 western-cell rate. A possible authoring error: confusing the annual rate (~0.28°/year) with a monthly rate (~0.3°/month).
sources_cited: Finlay et al. 2020, The CHAOS-7 geomagnetic field model and observed changes in the South Atlantic Anomaly, Earth Planets Space 72:156 (https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40623-020-01252-9)
CSES proton-flux SAA drift study, Science China Earth Sciences 2025 (https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11430-025-1672-2)
routing_notes: Refinement only — decider should apply proposed_field_updates and replace detail_reasoning and tldr on the existing PRED-073 entry, no new entry. review_class verification because work scope is quantitative refinement of an existing analysis (batchable per PROP-025). NARRATE-CITE: every paragraph in proposed_detail_reasoning_replacement and proposed_tldr contains at least one inline citation per PROP-014 Mech 3.
newsworthy: Dome’s SAA drift prediction (PRED-073) is ~12× faster than the actual IGRF-13 secular variation — and the dome’s own falsification threshold is already triggered by standard physics. Likely to fail on the dome’s own rule when May CHAOS-7 data lands in June.
